---
title: "Model Checking and Validation"
format: html
engine: julia
order: 4
---

# Introduction

You've learned how to fit models and diagnose MCMC convergence. But a converged chain doesn't mean your model is *correct* - it just means the sampler has explored the posterior properly. This session focuses on a different question: **does your model actually describe the data well?**

Model checking is about building trust in your inferences. A model that doesn't generate data resembling your observations shouldn't be trusted to make predictions or inform policy.

# Objectives

In this session you will:

1. Use prior predictive checks to validate model specification
2. Use posterior predictive checks to assess model fit
3. Interpret residual patterns to diagnose model problems
4. Understand simulation-based calibration for validating inference pipelines

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}

# Setup

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| include: false

using Pkg
project_root = joinpath(@__DIR__, "..")
Pkg.activate(project_root)
```

## Source file

The source file of this session is located at `sessions/model_checking.qmd`.

## Load libraries

```{julia}
using Random
using Distributions
using DifferentialEquations
using DataFrames
using Plots
using StatsPlots
using Turing
using MCMCChains
using CSV
using DrWatson
using StatsBase
```

## Initialisation

```{julia}
Random.seed!(1234)
```

:::

# The Bayesian workflow

Model checking isn't a one-time step at the end - it's part of an iterative workflow:

1. **Specify model** - choose structure, priors, likelihood
2. **Prior predictive check** - do simulations from priors look reasonable?
3. **Fit model** - run MCMC or other inference
4. **Diagnose sampler** - check convergence (previous session)
5. **Posterior predictive check** - does the fitted model generate realistic data?
6. **Iterate** - if checks fail, revise the model

We've covered steps 1-4. This session focuses on steps 2, 5, and 6.

# Prior predictive checks

Before fitting any data, you should ask: *do my priors make sense?* Prior predictive checking simulates data purely from your prior assumptions, without conditioning on observations.

Let's use the SIR model from the introduction:

```{julia}
function sir_ode!(du, u, p, t)
    R_0, D_inf = p
    β = R_0 / D_inf
    γ = 1.0 / D_inf
    S, I, R, C = u
    N = S + I + R

    du[1] = -β * S * I / N
    du[2] = β * S * I / N - γ * I
    du[3] = γ * I
    du[4] = β * S * I / N  # Cumulative incidence
end

function simulate_sir(R_0, D_inf, S0, I0, times)
    u0 = [S0, I0, 0.0, 0.0]
    prob = ODEProblem(sir_ode!, u0, (times[1], times[end]), [R_0, D_inf])
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=times)

    inc_cumulative = [sol[i][4] for i in 1:length(sol)]
    inc_daily = [0.0; diff(inc_cumulative)]

    return inc_daily
end
```

Now let's define a model with priors and sample from the prior predictive distribution:

```{julia}
@model function sir_model(obs, times, S0, I0)
    # Priors
    R_0 ~ Uniform(0.5, 10.0)
    D_inf ~ Uniform(1.0, 14.0)
    ρ ~ Uniform(0.0, 1.0)  # Reporting rate

    # Simulate
    inc = simulate_sir(R_0, D_inf, S0, I0, times)

    # Likelihood
    for i in eachindex(obs)
        λ = max(ρ * inc[i], 1e-10)
        obs[i] ~ Poisson(λ)
    end
end
```

```{julia}
# Prior predictive sampling
times = 0.0:1.0:59.0
S0, I0 = 279.0, 5.0

# Sample from priors (no data conditioning)
prior_samples = sample(sir_model(fill(missing, 60), times, S0, I0), Prior(), 200, progress=false)
```

```{julia}
# Generate trajectories from prior samples
p_prior = plot(title="Prior Predictive Check",
               xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Expected daily cases",
               legend=false)

for i in 1:100
    R_0 = prior_samples[:R_0][i]
    D_inf = prior_samples[:D_inf][i]
    ρ = prior_samples[:ρ][i]

    inc = simulate_sir(R_0, D_inf, S0, I0, times)
    obs_expected = ρ .* inc
    plot!(p_prior, collect(times), obs_expected, alpha=0.2, color=:blue)
end

plot!(p_prior, ylim=(0, 150))
```

**Questions to ask:**

- Do the simulated epidemics look plausible given what you know about influenza?
- Is the range of peak sizes reasonable?
- Are there impossible trajectories (negative values, explosions)?

If prior predictive simulations look unreasonable, revise your priors before fitting data. This is much easier than debugging a fitted model.

::: {.callout-tip}
## When priors are too vague
If your prior predictive check shows wildly varying trajectories - some with 0 cases, others with thousands - your priors may be too vague. Consider using domain knowledge to constrain them. For influenza, $R_0$ between 1 and 5 is more realistic than 0.5 to 10.
:::

# Posterior predictive checks

After fitting the model, posterior predictive checks ask: *can the fitted model generate data that looks like what we observed?*

```{julia}
# Load data and fit model
flu_tdc = CSV.read(datadir("flu_tdc_1971.csv"), DataFrame)

model = sir_model(flu_tdc.obs, times, S0, I0)
chain = sample(model, NUTS(), 1000, progress=false)
```

```{julia}
# Generate posterior predictive samples
function posterior_predictive(chain, times, S0, I0, n_sims=100)
    sims = []
    n_samples = length(chain)
    indices = rand(1:n_samples, n_sims)

    for idx in indices
        R_0 = chain[:R_0][idx]
        D_inf = chain[:D_inf][idx]
        ρ = chain[:ρ][idx]

        inc = simulate_sir(R_0, D_inf, S0, I0, times)
        # Sample from observation model
        obs_sim = rand.(Poisson.(max.(ρ .* inc, 1e-10)))
        push!(sims, obs_sim)
    end
    return sims
end

ppc_samples = posterior_predictive(chain, times, S0, I0)
```

```{julia}
# Plot posterior predictive check
p_ppc = plot(title="Posterior Predictive Check",
             xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Daily cases")

for sim in ppc_samples
    plot!(p_ppc, collect(times), sim, alpha=0.1, color=:lightblue, label="")
end

scatter!(p_ppc, flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs,
         color=:red, label="Observed", markersize=4)
plot!(p_ppc, legend=:topright)
```

**What to look for:**

- Do the simulated data envelopes contain the observations?
- Are there systematic patterns the model misses?
- Does the model capture the variability in the data?

For the Tristan da Cunha data with a simple SIR model, you'll likely see the model fails to capture the **second wave**. This is expected - SIR doesn't have temporary immunity. This motivates the SEITL/SEIT4L models in later sessions.

# Quantifying posterior predictive fit

Visual checks are valuable but subjective. We can also compute summary statistics to quantify fit.

```{julia}
# Compute summary statistics for each posterior predictive sample
function compute_summaries(trajectory)
    peak_value = maximum(trajectory)
    peak_time = argmax(trajectory) - 1  # Convert to 0-indexed time
    total_cases = sum(trajectory)
    return (peak=peak_value, peak_time=peak_time, total=total_cases)
end

# Summaries for observed data
obs_summary = compute_summaries(flu_tdc.obs)
println("Observed: peak=$(obs_summary.peak), peak_time=$(obs_summary.peak_time), total=$(obs_summary.total)")

# Summaries for posterior predictive samples
ppc_summaries = [compute_summaries(sim) for sim in ppc_samples]
ppc_peaks = [s.peak for s in ppc_summaries]
ppc_peak_times = [s.peak_time for s in ppc_summaries]
ppc_totals = [s.total for s in ppc_summaries]
```

```{julia}
# Plot distributions of summary statistics
p1 = histogram(ppc_peaks, bins=20, alpha=0.7, label="Posterior pred.",
               xlabel="Peak cases", ylabel="Count", title="Peak Size")
vline!(p1, [obs_summary.peak], color=:red, linewidth=2, label="Observed")

p2 = histogram(ppc_peak_times, bins=20, alpha=0.7, label="Posterior pred.",
               xlabel="Peak time (days)", ylabel="Count", title="Peak Timing")
vline!(p2, [obs_summary.peak_time], color=:red, linewidth=2, label="Observed")

p3 = histogram(ppc_totals, bins=20, alpha=0.7, label="Posterior pred.",
               xlabel="Total cases", ylabel="Count", title="Total Cases")
vline!(p3, [obs_summary.total], color=:red, linewidth=2, label="Observed")

plot(p1, p2, p3, layout=(1, 3), size=(900, 300), legend=:topright)
```

If the observed value (red line) falls in the tails of the posterior predictive distribution, the model struggles to explain that aspect of the data.

# Residual analysis

Residuals are the difference between observed and predicted values. Patterns in residuals can reveal model deficiencies.

```{julia}
# Compute posterior mean predictions
mean_R_0 = mean(chain[:R_0])
mean_D_inf = mean(chain[:D_inf])
mean_ρ = mean(chain[:ρ])

mean_inc = simulate_sir(mean_R_0, mean_D_inf, S0, I0, times)
mean_pred = mean_ρ .* mean_inc

# Compute residuals
residuals = flu_tdc.obs .- mean_pred
```

```{julia}
# Plot residuals over time
p_resid = plot(collect(times), residuals,
               xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Residual (obs - pred)",
               title="Residuals Over Time", legend=false,
               marker=:circle, markersize=3)
hline!(p_resid, [0], color=:black, linestyle=:dash)
```

**What patterns indicate problems?**

- **Trend**: residuals systematically positive or negative over time → model missing temporal structure
- **Autocorrelation**: residuals at time $t$ predict residuals at $t+1$ → missing dynamics
- **Heteroscedasticity**: residual variance changes with predicted value → wrong observation model

For the SIR fit to Tristan da Cunha, you should see clear structure: positive residuals during the second wave that the SIR model can't capture.

```{julia}
# Check for autocorrelation in residuals
lag1_resid = residuals[1:end-1]
lag0_resid = residuals[2:end]

p_acf = scatter(lag1_resid, lag0_resid,
                xlabel="Residual at t", ylabel="Residual at t+1",
                title="Residual Autocorrelation", legend=false,
                alpha=0.6)
```

Strong correlation here suggests the model is missing important dynamics - exactly what we'd expect when fitting SIR to two-wave data.

# Simulation-based calibration

A more rigorous check asks: *does my inference procedure recover true parameters when I know them?*

Simulation-based calibration (SBC) works as follows:

1. Draw parameters from the prior: $\theta^* \sim p(\theta)$
2. Simulate data from the model: $y^* \sim p(y|\theta^*)$
3. Fit the model to $y^*$ and get posterior samples
4. Check if $\theta^*$ falls within the posterior as expected

If your inference is correct, the true parameter should be uniformly distributed within the posterior samples (i.e., the 95% credible interval should contain the truth 95% of the time).

```{julia}
#| output: false
# SBC for R_0 (simplified example with fewer iterations for speed)
function run_sbc(n_sbc=20)
    ranks = Int[]

    for i in 1:n_sbc
        # 1. Draw from prior
        R_0_true = rand(Uniform(0.5, 10.0))
        D_inf_true = rand(Uniform(1.0, 14.0))
        ρ_true = rand(Uniform(0.0, 1.0))

        # 2. Simulate data
        inc = simulate_sir(R_0_true, D_inf_true, S0, I0, times)
        obs_sim = rand.(Poisson.(max.(ρ_true .* inc, 1e-10)))

        # 3. Fit model (short chain for speed)
        model_sim = sir_model(obs_sim, times, S0, I0)
        chain_sim = sample(model_sim, NUTS(), 200, progress=false)

        # 4. Compute rank of true value
        R_0_samples = vec(chain_sim[:R_0])
        rank = sum(R_0_samples .< R_0_true)
        push!(ranks, rank)
    end

    return ranks
end

# This takes a while - in practice you'd run more iterations
sbc_ranks = run_sbc(20)
```

```{julia}
# Plot SBC histogram
histogram(sbc_ranks, bins=10,
          xlabel="Rank", ylabel="Count",
          title="SBC Rank Histogram for R_0",
          legend=false)
```

A uniform histogram indicates well-calibrated inference. Peaks at the edges suggest the posterior is too narrow (overconfident) or too wide (underconfident).

::: {.callout-note}
## SBC in practice
Full SBC requires many simulations (100+) and is computationally expensive. It's most valuable when developing new models or inference methods. For routine analysis, prior and posterior predictive checks are usually sufficient.
:::

# When models fail checks

What should you do when model checks reveal problems?

**Prior predictive failures:**

- Priors too vague → use domain knowledge to constrain
- Impossible values → add bounds or change distribution family
- Missing structure → reconsider model specification

**Posterior predictive failures:**

- Systematic bias → model is missing important mechanisms
- Poor coverage → observation model may be wrong (e.g., Poisson vs Negative Binomial)
- Specific features missed → consider model extensions

**Residual patterns:**

- Temporal trends → add time-varying parameters or additional compartments
- Autocorrelation → model dynamics are incomplete
- Heteroscedasticity → observation model variance structure is wrong

The key insight is that **model failure is information**. A model that fails to capture the second wave in Tristan da Cunha tells us something important: simple SIR dynamics are insufficient, motivating the temporary immunity models we explore in later sessions.

::: {.callout-tip title="Learning points"}
- **Prior predictive checks** validate that your priors generate plausible data before you see observations
- **Posterior predictive checks** test whether the fitted model can reproduce key features of the observed data
- **Residual patterns** diagnose specific model deficiencies: trends, autocorrelation, heteroscedasticity
- **Simulation-based calibration** rigorously tests whether inference recovers known parameters
- **Model failure is informative** - it guides model improvement and reveals missing mechanisms
:::

# Going further

## Posterior predictive p-values

For any test statistic $T(y)$, you can compute a posterior predictive p-value:

$$p = P(T(y^{rep}) \geq T(y^{obs}) | y^{obs})$$

Values near 0 or 1 indicate the model struggles to reproduce that aspect of the data.

## Cross-validation

Leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO-CV) estimates out-of-sample predictive performance. In Julia, [ParetoSmooth.jl](https://github.com/TuringLang/ParetoSmooth.jl) provides efficient PSIS-LOO for Turing models.

## Calibration plots

For models predicting probabilities or intervals, calibration plots check if predicted 90% intervals contain the truth 90% of the time across many predictions.

# Next session

In [Observation Models](observation_models.qmd), we'll explore different ways to connect model predictions to data - from Poisson likelihoods to Negative Binomial and even likelihood-free approaches like ABC.

# References

- Gelman et al. (2013). [Bayesian Data Analysis](http://www.stat.columbia.edu/~gelman/book/), Chapter 6. (Comprehensive treatment of model checking)
- Gabry et al. (2019). [Visualization in Bayesian workflow](https://arxiv.org/abs/1709.01449). *JRSS A* 182(2), 389-402.
- Talts et al. (2018). [Validating Bayesian Inference Algorithms with Simulation-Based Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.06788). arXiv.
- [Turing.jl documentation](https://turinglang.org/stable/) - Prior and posterior predictive sampling
